preFix.title: Coil-built large statement vase
preFix.subtitle: A tall air-dry coil vase with a wide belly and narrowed neck
preFix.excerpt: A coil-built vase approximately 300 mm tall with a wide belly and a narrowed neck. Air-dry clay throughout, no kiln required. The form is built in stages over two days to allow the lower coils to stiffen before the upper section is added. Suitable as a decorative vessel for dried flowers or sculptural branches. Not waterproof.
preFix.sourceNotes: Coil-building is one of the oldest hand-building methods. Technique derived from first principles. Encyclopedia Britannica 11th edition (1911), pottery entry, for historical coil-building context.

content.text: A large coil-built vase is one of the most satisfying projects in hand-building. The form is built up from individual
coil

s of clay stacked on a pinch-pot base, then blended into walls. At this scale, the build happens in two sessions: the lower half is allowed to firm up before the upper half is added, so the belly does not collapse under its own weight. No kiln is required. Air-dry clay is not waterproof; use the finished vase for dried flowers or sculptural branches, not fresh stems in water.
Session one: base and lower half
Start with a base disc. Pinch a ball of clay (approximately 150 g) into a flat disc 100 mm in diameter and 8 mm thick. This is the vase base. Set it on the wooden board.
Roll coils on a smooth work surface by pressing down and moving your palms outward. Each coil should be about 12 mm in diameter and long enough to circle the base once. Apply
score and slip
to the top edge of the base and the underside of the first coil. Press the coil into position and blend the joint into the interior wall with a fingertip. Smooth the exterior with the wooden rib.

Continue stacking coils to build up the lower section, about 150 mm tall. For the widening belly, each successive coil is placed slightly outward from the one below it. Tilt each coil outward by about 5 mm before blending the joint. For a vase with a belly at roughly the midpoint, the widest point should be around 130 mm in diameter.
After the first 150 mm of height, cover the top edge with a damp cloth and leave the lower half to firm to
leather-hard
before continuing. This takes 2-4 hours.
Session two: upper half and neck

Once the lower half is firm, resume building. For the upper half, gradually angle each new coil inward to bring the walls back toward the vertical for a narrowing neck. Place each coil about 5 mm inward from the one below. Build up to a total height of 300 mm, with the neck narrowing to about 60 mm in diameter at the mouth.

The neck coils do not need to be blended to a smooth surface if a textured exterior is desired. Leaving the coil marks visible is a valid finish choice. For a smooth exterior, blend each coil as it is added using the wooden rib.
Finishing the rim
The top coil forms the rim. Roll a coil of even diameter and join it as usual, then compress the top edge with a damp fingertip to close any gaps and create a clean finish.
Drying
Allow the completed vase to dry slowly under a loose plastic bag for the first 24 hours, then remove the cover and allow it to reach
bone-dry
over a further 3-5 days. Large pieces dry more slowly at their thickest sections. Check the base for cracks daily; a crack at the base-to-wall join is the most common failure point.
Finishing
Sand rough patches lightly. Apply two coats of clear acrylic varnish. For a coloured finish, paint with acrylic paint before varnishing.
Care

Air-dry clay is not waterproof. Do not add water to the vase. For dried flowers or branches, the finished piece performs well. For a vase that will hold fresh flowers, fit a glass tube or small jam jar inside the neck.
Variations
Leave the coil texture visible on the exterior by blending only the interior surfaces.
Build to a wider, shorter profile: a belly of 180 mm diameter at 200 mm total height gives a rounder, more squat form.
Add handles: attach two pulled clay handles at the widest belly point once the lower half is leather-hard.

rows.fix: Support the exterior with crumpled paper or foam wedges while the belly firms up. Resume building only when the lower half feels solid.
rows.cause: The lower section was not firm enough before the upper section was added
rows.problem: Walls bulge outward at the belly

rows.fix: Mend with fresh slip. Blend all joins on the interior even if the exterior texture is left rough.
rows.cause: Insufficient score and slip between coils, or coils dried out before blending

rows.fix: Keep the base covered with plastic until the first coil is added. Score and slip both surfaces before pressing.
rows.cause: The base disc had dried more than the first coil before joining
rows.problem: Base separates from the lowest coil

rows.fix: Check the base is flat before building. Use a level if available. Press the damp base disc flat before it stiffens.
rows.cause: The base disc is not flat, or coils were placed unevenly

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
